questions.question_text: Which statement best describes force in exploring forces?

options.text: A contact force acts when two objects are touching.
A force is a push or pull that can change an object's motion or shape.
A non-contact force acts without the objects touching.
Balanced forces cancel in their combined effect and do not change an object's motion.

questions.explanation: A force is a push or pull that can change an object's motion or shape.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 5; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with force?

options.text: Earth's gravity pulls a falling stone downward.
Friction acts between a shoe and the ground during walking.
Pushing a box can make it start moving.
A book resting on a table has upward support balancing its weight.

questions.explanation: Pushing a box can make it start moving.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 5; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes force from a nearby idea?

options.text: Balanced forces can act on an object that is already moving at constant speed.
Contact is required for forces such as friction and a push by hand.
Gravity, magnetic force, and electrostatic force can act at a distance.
A force is an interaction, so it involves more than simply naming an object.

questions.explanation: A force is an interaction, so it involves more than simply naming an object.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 5; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of force?

options.text: Draw the direction of the push or pull when explaining a change in motion.
Increase or reduce contact friction depending on the task and safety need.
Predict an interaction even when the objects are separated.
Compare the directions and sizes of forces before deciding whether they are balanced.

questions.explanation: Draw the direction of the push or pull when explaining a change in motion.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 5; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about force?

options.text: A contact force cannot act across an empty gap.
A force does not always make an object move if other forces balance it.
Lack of touching does not mean that no force is acting.
Balanced does not always mean that no forces are present.

questions.explanation: A force does not always make an object move if other forces balance it.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 5; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A learner is explaining force. Which description is most accurate?

options.text: A non-contact force acts without the objects touching.
A contact force acts when two objects are touching.
A force is a push or pull that can change an object's motion or shape.
Balanced forces cancel in their combined effect and do not change an object's motion.

questions.explanation: A force is a push or pull that can change an object's motion or shape.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 5; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which observation would provide useful evidence about force?

options.text: A book resting on a table has upward support balancing its weight.
Friction acts between a shoe and the ground during walking.
Earth's gravity pulls a falling stone downward.
Pushing a box can make it start moving.

questions.explanation: Pushing a box can make it start moving.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 5; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

options.text: Draw the direction of the push or pull when explaining a change in motion.
Increase or reduce contact friction depending on the task and safety need.
Predict an interaction even when the objects are separated.
Compare the directions and sizes of forces before deciding whether they are balanced.

options.text: Contact is required for forces such as friction and a push by hand.

options.text: Predict an interaction even when the objects are separated.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes contact forces in exploring forces?

options.text: Unbalanced forces have a non-zero combined effect and can change motion.
A non-contact force acts without the objects touching.
Balanced forces cancel in their combined effect and do not change an object's motion.
A contact force acts when two objects are touching.

questions.explanation: A contact force acts when two objects are touching.

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with contact forces?

options.text: Friction acts between a shoe and the ground during walking.
Earth's gravity pulls a falling stone downward.
A book resting on a table has upward support balancing its weight.
A stronger push can make a stationary trolley accelerate.

questions.explanation: Friction acts between a shoe and the ground during walking.

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes contact forces from a nearby idea?

options.text: Gravity, magnetic force, and electrostatic force can act at a distance.
Contact is required for forces such as friction and a push by hand.
Balanced forces can act on an object that is already moving at constant speed.

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of contact forces?

options.text: Compare the directions and sizes of forces before deciding whether they are balanced.
Predict an interaction even when the objects are separated.
Increase or reduce contact friction depending on the task and safety need.
Look for acceleration or a change in direction as evidence of an unbalanced force.

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about contact forces?

options.text: A large force is not unbalanced if an equal opposing force cancels it.
Lack of touching does not mean that no force is acting.
Balanced does not always mean that no forces are present.
A contact force cannot act across an empty gap.

questions.explanation: A contact force cannot act across an empty gap.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 5; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

options.text: A contact force acts when two objects are touching.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes non-contact forces in exploring forces?

options.text: Balanced forces cancel in their combined effect and do not change an object's motion.
A non-contact force acts without the objects touching.
Unbalanced forces have a non-zero combined effect and can change motion.
Gravity is an attractive force between masses and gives objects weight near Earth.

questions.explanation: A non-contact force acts without the objects touching.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 5; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with non-contact forces?

options.text: A stronger push can make a stationary trolley accelerate.
A book resting on a table has upward support balancing its weight.
Earth's gravity pulls a falling stone downward.
A dropped ball accelerates toward the ground.

options.text: Mass describes the amount of matter, while weight is the gravitational force on that mass.

Gravity, magnetic force, and electrostatic force can act at a distance.

options.text: Predict an interaction even when the objects are separated.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes balanced forces in exploring forces?

options.text: Friction opposes relative motion between surfaces that are in contact.
Unbalanced forces have a non-zero combined effect and can change motion.
Gravity is an attractive force between masses and gives objects weight near Earth.
Balanced forces cancel in their combined effect and do not change an object's motion.

questions.explanation: Balanced forces cancel in their combined effect and do not change an object's motion.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 5; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with balanced forces?

options.text: A book resting on a table has upward support balancing its weight.
A stronger push can make a stationary trolley accelerate.
A dropped ball accelerates toward the ground.
Shoe soles need friction with the ground to prevent slipping.

questions.explanation: A book resting on a table has upward support balancing its weight.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 5; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes balanced forces from a nearby idea?

options.text: An unbalanced force may change speed, direction, or both.
Balanced forces can act on an object that is already moving at constant speed.
Mass describes the amount of matter, while weight is the gravitational force on that mass.
Friction can be useful for control and harmful when it causes unwanted wear or heating.

questions.explanation: Balanced forces can act on an object that is already moving at constant speed.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 5; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of balanced forces?

options.text: Use gravity to explain falling and the support force to explain an object at rest.
Look for acceleration or a change in direction as evidence of an unbalanced force.
Compare the directions and sizes of forces before deciding whether they are balanced.
Add tread or roughness when grip is needed and lubrication when rubbing should be reduced.

questions.explanation: Compare the directions and sizes of forces before deciding whether they are balanced.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 5; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about balanced forces?

options.text: Removing all friction would make walking and braking impossible.
A large force is not unbalanced if an equal opposing force cancels it.

Balanced does not always mean that no forces are present.

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about friction?

options.text: All metals are not attracted equally to a magnet.
Removing all friction would make walking and braking impossible.
An attracted object is not necessarily charged with the opposite sign; polarisation can also matter.
A force diagram should not include every object in the room.

questions.explanation: Removing all friction would make walking and braking impossible.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 5; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A learner is explaining friction. Which description is most accurate?

options.text: Electrostatic force is an attraction or repulsion between electrically charged objects.
Magnetic force can attract or repel certain magnetic materials and magnets without contact.
Friction opposes relative motion between surfaces that are in contact.
A force diagram represents the important forces on an object with labelled arrows.

questions.explanation: Friction opposes relative motion between surfaces that are in contact.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 5; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which observation would provide useful evidence about friction?

options.text: A free-body diagram can show weight downward and support upward on a book.
A magnet can attract an iron pin through a sheet of paper.
A rubbed balloon can attract small paper pieces.
Shoe soles need friction with the ground to prevent slipping.

questions.explanation: Shoe soles need friction with the ground to prevent slipping.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 5; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which action is the most appropriate use of the idea of friction?

options.text: Add tread or roughness when grip is needed and lubrication when rubbing should be reduced.
Use attraction and repulsion between poles to test a magnet safely.
Use a dry, safe demonstration to compare attraction after rubbing different materials.
Start a force explanation by drawing only the forces relevant to the chosen object.

questions.explanation: Add tread or roughness when grip is needed and lubrication when rubbing should be reduced.

questions.question_text: A class records an observation related to friction. Which interpretation is best supported?

options.text: Magnetic force depends on the materials and poles involved, not on every metal being magnetic.
Friction can be useful for control and harmful when it causes unwanted wear or heating.
Electrostatic effects involve charge imbalance and can occur without continuous current in a circuit.
Arrow direction and relative size communicate the combined effect of forces.

questions.explanation: Friction can be useful for control and harmful when it causes unwanted wear or heating.
